title: Dormant Card Reactivation in High-Risk Currency Market
narrative: A card with 8-14 months of transaction dormancy suddenly generates 6-12 CNP purchases totaling $840-$1,950 within 72 hours, all denominated in a foreign currency (PHP, THB, or IDR) the cardholder never previously used. Transactions occur exclusively through digital goods MCCs (5816, 5815) with 4-6 different merchant IDs clustered in the same country. The card1_txn_count_24hr spikes from 0 to 8+ while card1_amt_zscore exceeds 4.2 due to the sharp departure from historical patterns. P_emaildomain switches to a free provider (yahoo.com, outlook.com) different from the original registration. All transactions occur during off-hours relative to the cardholder's addr1 timezone (txn_hour between 2-6 AM local time), and dist1 values show consistent 8,000-11,000 km separation from billing address.
indicators: Card dormant 240+ days, then 6-12 transactions in 72 hours all in previously unused currency
All transactions digital goods MCCs (5816/5815) in single foreign country (PHP/THB/IDR denomination)
P_emaildomain changed to free provider not matching original registration domain
100% of transactions occur txn_hour 2-6 AM relative to addr1 timezone
dist1 consistently 8,000-11,000 km, indicating persistent foreign IP geolocation
Total spend $840-$1,950 across 4-6 unique merchant IDs in same country
summary: A card inactive for 8-14 months suddenly executes 6-12 digital goods purchases in 72 hours, all in a foreign currency never used before (Philippine peso, Thai baht, or Indonesian rupiah), with transactions happening exclusively during the cardholder's local nighttime hours from IPs 8,000+ kilometers away.
reasoning: Fraudsters who acquired stolen card credentials are testing and exploiting the card through low-friction digital goods merchants in Southeast Asian markets, betting that dormant accounts receive less monitoring and that the cardholder won't notice until after maximum damage is done.
indicator_explanations: Card sits unused for eight months then suddenly fires multiple times in unfamiliar currency
Card goes from zero activity to eight transactions in one day with abnormally high amounts
All purchases are digital goods from a single foreign country using exotic currency
Email address switched to generic free provider completely different from original registration
Every transaction happens in the middle of the night for the cardholder's home location
IP address stays locked in foreign country thousands of miles from billing address
Total damage ranges from eight hundred to two thousand dollars across multiple foreign merchants
